content:
1. Introduction
Electrodialysis (ED) is an electrically driven separation process using ion exchange membranes (IEM); it is largely applied during 60 years [1]. H. Strathmann characterizes ED as a mature technology, which still has technical and commercial limitations. There is a substantial need for basic as well as applied research to improve the processes involved [1]. In particular, intensive electric regimes are of great interest as they result in decreasing the area of expensive IEMs and lower investment costs. New ideas coming from other areas of knowledge, namely, from micro- and nanofluidics [2], allows one to revise the conventional layout of ED and to propose novel conceptions of electro-membrane devices for water desalination using extended space charge as a barrier for ion transfer [3] or the effect of “deionization shock” in microstructures such as porous frits [4,5]. Relatively high voltages applied in these new devices give rise to phenomena similar to those occurring in intensive ED with IEMs, involving coupling of current and water transfer in solution.
Schematically, a conventional ED elementary cell is shown in Fig. 1. It includes an anion-exchange membrane (AEM), a cation-exchange membrane (CEM) and solutions flowing between them. When an electric current passes through an ion-selective membrane separating two electrolyte solutions, salt concentration decreases at one membrane side and increases at the other due to selective transport of counterions in the membrane. In membrane science, the emergence of concentration gradients at membrane/solution interface induced by transmembrane driving forces is called “concentration polarization” (CP) [6]. In electrochemistry, this term denotes the part of the polarization of an electrolytic cell resulting from changes in the electrolyte concentration due to the passage of current through the solution [7]. Here polarization is understood as the shift of the potential difference (PD) across the cell from its equilibrium value, equivalent to “concentration overpotential” [8].

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
On the one hand, CP phenomenon results in solution desalination and concentration during ED. On the other hand, CP leads to higher energy consumption and to rate limitation of the process. Theoretically, under local electroneutrality (LEN) assumption, the current density, i, is limited by a value (the limiting current density, ilim) that corresponds to zero electrolyte concentration at the surface of one of the membranes forming the desalting channel: ilim = D c0 F / [δ (T1 – t1)], where D is the electrolyte diffusion coefficient, c0 is the electrolyte concentration in the solution bulk, t1 is the counterion transport number in solution, T1 is the integral counterion transport number in the membrane (usually close to 1), and δ is the Nernst diffusion boundary layer (DBL) thickness defined as the distance from the membrane to the cross point of the tangents drown to the concentration profile at the interface and bulk solution. Eq. (1) was first obtained by Peers in 1956 [10]. When studying 1D transport, δ is considered as a fitting parameter. In 2D modeling under LEN assumption (the Nernst–Planck – Navier–Stokes (NP/NS) model) [11,12], δ can be found as a function of the distance from the channel entrance, L; the average Hagen–Poiseuille flow velocity through the channel, V0, and the inter-membranes distance, h. For sufficiently small L (short-length channels) the Lévêque approximation [13,14] is valid: δ ≈ 1.02 (L D h / V0)^(1/3).
The I–V curve calculated using the 2D NP/NS model has a quasi-linear region at low concentration polarization, and a horizontal plateau corresponding to ilim. However, in practice, “overlimiting current” is always observed. There are several mechanisms of the overlimiting conductance discussed for decades [15–32]. They involve water splitting resulting in occurrence of new charge carriers, H+ and OH- ions [15,21]. In addition to the contribution of these ions in the transfer of electric current, they affect electric field in the depleted diffusion layer that causes an increase in the counterion current (the Kharkats effect of current exaltation) [20,21,23]. There are also two types of coupled convection [15,21], the gravitational one [21–27] and the electroconvection (EC) [17–19,21–32], and some other effects [32,33]. Experimental and theoretical evidences [17,22–31,34–36] show that in dilute solutions the overlimiting transfer is mainly due to electroconvection, which generally occurs as current-induced volume transport when an electric field is imposed through the charged solution [37].

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
Physical and mathematical modeling of overlimiting transfer is a major challenge due to multi-physics and multi-scale nature of the problem. 1D models [23,38–40] inspired by Rubinstein and Shtilman [17] use the Poisson equation instead of “classical” LEN condition. It was shown that when i approaches ilim, the counterion concentration at the membrane surface does not tend to zero; instead, an extended space charge region (SCR) much larger than the equilibrium electric double layer (EDL) appears, thus reducing the electroneutral DBL. However, the decrease in effective DBL thickness is too small to explain the experimentally observed growth in overlimiting current. To fit experimental I–V curves, δ must be adjusted as a function of i. The physics behind 1D models cannot explain the effect of variation of δ or give another plausible explanation of overlimiting ion transfer.
In fact, 2D modeling involving Navier–Stokes equations is needed. Dukhin and Mishchuk [41], and Rubinstein et al. [42] were the first to give a qualitative explanation of overlimiting current by electroconvection. Two conditions must be fulfilled: (i) an extended SCR should be formed, far beyond the equilibrium EDL, and (ii) the distribution of electric field lines should be inhomogeneous to give rise to a spatially inhomogeneous electric bulk force. In the cases considered in [41,42], the second condition was realized due to spherical geometry of ion-exchange particles [41] or due to heterogeneous membrane surface [42]. Dukhin and Mishchuk introduced the term “electroosmosis of the second kind” [41,43] to emphasize the difference from classical electroosmosis (first kind) caused by the action of the external electric field on the equilibrium EDL, while electroconvection studied here is caused by the action of the electric field on the extended SCR. Later, Dukhin and Mishchuk [44,45], and Rubinstein [46] found analytical approximate solutions for the Navier–Stokes equations coupled to the Nernst–Planck–Poisson equation (NPP/NS) in ion conducting heterogeneous systems: in solution/spherical ion-exchange particle [44] and solution/heterogeneous ion-exchange membranes [45,46].

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
In 2000, Rubinstein and Zaltzman [18] studied mass transfer in a solution between two homogeneous ion-exchange membranes without forced flow. They found a numerical solution for NP/NS equations under LEN assumption except for the boundary SCR, using a special slip condition deduced from asymptotic theory of non-equilibrium EDL. They showed that surface heterogeneity is not necessary for rising electroconvection; hydrodynamic instability at sufficiently high voltages leads to emergence of vortices. Similar approach was applied by Dydek et al. [47] and by Green and Yossifon [48] using Helmholtz–Smoluchowski slip condition. Note that these theoretical studies involve no forced convection, which does not conform to the practice of electrodialysis. In Refs [21,54] forced convection was taken into account, but coupling between Navier–Stokes and Nernst–Planck equations was only partial; the electric body force was approximately calculated using Rubinstein's 1D model.
Kwak et al. [30] recently studied theoretically and experimentally the balance of forced convection (Hagen–Poiseuille shear flow) and electroconvection in an ED channel between an AEM and a CEM. They found deviation from pair symmetry of EC vortices, tendency to unidirectional vortex structure, and a new scaling law for vortex size as a function of potential drop and forced flow velocity, with good agreement between experiment and theory.
In this paper, we propose a 2D model of overlimiting ion transfer in an ED membrane channel with both forced and current-induced convection, in the absence of chemical effects. The model is based on fully coupled Navier–Stokes and Nernst–Planck–Poisson equations and contains no adjustable parameters. The numerical solution is found without a priori separation into electroneutral and charged regions. While NPP/NS equations are largely used in electrokinetics and nano/microfluidics [30,56,57], certain peculiarities, such as specific boundary conditions, distinguish our model from earlier ones.

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
2. Theoretical – Governing equations and boundary conditions
The system is sketched in Fig. 1. Let x and y be the transverse and longitudinal coordinates; x=0 relates to AEM/solution boundary, x=h to the boundary with CEM; y=0 is inlet, y=L outlet of the desalting channel. For a 1:1 electrolyte in the absence of chemical reactions, the Nernst–Planck equations for ion fluxes, continuity equations, Poisson equation, electric current density, Navier–Stokes equation with electric body force, and continuity for fluid are used. The electric body force is f = F(c1 – c2)∇φ. Boundary conditions at membrane/solution interfaces: ideally selective membranes (counterion current equals total current, co‑ion normal flux zero), no‑slip condition (fluid velocity zero at walls), and Dirichlet–Rubinstein conditions for counterion concentrations at interfaces (c1m = Nc c0 at CEM, c2m = Na c0 at AEM, with Nc, Na being parameters). The potential difference (PD) across the cell pair Δφtot is the sum of PD in the desalting compartment and the contributions from AEM and CEM half‑cells including Donnan potentials and ohmic drops. The total PD is set as a linear function of time (potentiodynamic mode). Inlet conditions: parabolic Poiseuille velocity profile, uniform concentration except at boundaries; outlet: “soft” conditions with zero tangential derivatives and parabolic velocity; initial conditions: uniform concentration (except at boundaries) and parabolic velocity. The system is solved using finite element method, iteratively coupling NPP and NS equations.

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Current–voltage curve
Simulations were performed for dilute NaCl solutions (10⁻⁴–10⁻⁵ M) in a flow‑through ED cell. The calculated I–V curve (Fig. 2) shows a linear initial part, then after an under‑limiting region the current grows above the “classical” limiting current ilim, forming a sloping plateau where current density smoothly increases with voltage, and finally a steeper region with oscillations. The point where the tangent to the initial part and the sloping plateau intersect is very close to ilim predicted by the Lévêque equation. No electroconvection occurs at under‑limiting currents; it starts when concentration polarization becomes sufficiently high. At PD around 0.2 V (ohmic component omitted), the diffusion and migration components of the counterion current density attain their “limiting” values while the convective component remains zero. Above 0.2 V up to about 1 V, the diffusion and migration components do not change, while the convective component grows due to stable electroconvective vortices, corresponding to the smooth sloping plateau. Beyond about 1 V, all three components begin to oscillate regularly with a period of about 1 s, and then become irregular.
3.2. Electroconvection modes
In a cell with forced flow, concentration distribution is not uniform along the channel: electrolyte concentration decreases and DBL thickness increases with distance from the inlet. This results in a tangential component of the electric field and a tangential body force directed towards the inlet, producing steady‑state electroconvective clockwise vortices near the CEM close to the inlet. This regime occurs at 0.3–0.95 V and is similar to the Dukhin–Mishchuk electrokinetic mode, but without membrane surface undulation or electric heterogeneity – the tangential field arises from the longitudinal non‑uniformity of concentration and DBL thickness. At higher voltages (above 0.95 V), unsteady vortices appear near the outlet (Rubinstein–Zaltzman instability), leading to oscillations on the I–V curve. The forced flow breaks the symmetry of vortex pairs: clockwise vortices on the CEM and counterclockwise on the AEM are larger than the opposite ones, matching the direction of the forced flow. Both steady and unsteady vortices coexist in a certain voltage range: near the inlet vortices remain stable, while near the outlet they become unsteady.

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
3.3. Oscillations
Experimental chronopotentiograms of a Nafion‑117 membrane show several typical periods of oscillation: dominant quasi‑regular oscillations with a period close to 1 s (due to appearance/disappearance of an electroconvective vortex near the probing point), larger periods of 5–10 s (convective transfer of vortices with the forced flow), and smaller periods (<0.1 s) from small vortices emerging within big ones. The calculated I–V curve also exhibits such oscillations, with amplitude of PD oscillations around 10 mV (for a cell pair, when measuring capillaries are in concentrating compartments).
3.4. Concentration and electrochemical potential profiles
Calculated concentration profiles (Fig. 8) show correct shapes compared to laser interferometry experiments. At low currents the profile is near‑linear; at overlimiting currents, an electroconvective vortex mixes the solution, decreasing the concentration gradient in the vortex zone, shifting the diffusion‑controlled region toward the bulk, and expanding the depletion zone. At high voltages, flat regions and even inversion of slope appear, oscillating with ~1 s period. The extended SCR thickness is 5–10 μm at i > 2 ilim. The quasi‑equilibrium EDL thickness (from the point of minimum counterion concentration to the membrane) is about 2 μm, in good agreement with an analytical formula. The electrochemical potential of the cation is nearly constant within the quasi‑equilibrium EDL at low voltages, but deviates at higher voltages. The Donnan equation approximately holds but overestimates the PD at high currents due to neglect of ohmic contribution.
3.5. Use of slip condition
Introducing fluid slip at the membrane surface via the Navier slip condition (uslip = b ∂Vy/∂n) shifts the I–V curve to higher current densities at high voltages, indicating that slip reduces viscous restrictions and facilitates electroconvection.

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells

content:
4. Conclusion
A “basic” mathematical model of ion transfer in an electrodialysis cell with cation‑ and anion‑exchange membranes is proposed, aimed at describing intensive current regimes. The model is built on first‑principle equations and contains no adjustable parameters. A set of fully coupled Nernst–Planck–Poisson and Navier–Stokes equations is solved numerically with Dirichlet–Rubinstein boundary conditions in potentiodynamic mode. For the first time, a realistic I–V curve having the same main features as experimental ones is calculated for an ED flow‑through cell. The linear initial part is followed by a sloping plateau situated above the “limiting” current. The smooth part of the plateau corresponds to stable electroconvective vortices arising from inhomogeneous current density caused by partial desalination along the channel. A tangential component of the current through the extended space charge region produces electroosmosis of the second kind (Dukhin–Mishchuk mode), but without requiring membrane surface heterogeneity. At higher voltages, unstable electroconvection (Rubinstein–Zaltzman instability) leads to oscillations and an increased slope of the I–V curve. Unsteady vortices first emerge near the outlet, then occupy the entire channel. Both electroconvective modes can coexist within a certain voltage range. The model gives new insight into electroconvection enhancement of ion transfer in flow‑through ED cells. It can be extended to describe effects of profiled surfaces, ion‑conductive spacers, water splitting, and hydrophobic slip. The case of hydrodynamic slip is considered as an example of such development.

citation.title: Basic mathematical model of overlimiting transfer enhanced by electroconvection in flow-through electrodialysis membrane cells
